macro_segments.title: The Status of Dried Vines in a Mortgage (Mashkanta de-Sura): The Abaye-Rava Dispute

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Case: Vines Drying Out During the Mortgage Period
Abaye's Position: Dried Wood Counts as Fruit
Rava's Position: Dried Vines Remain Principal, Must Be Converted to Land
Proof from Baraita: When Vines Dry in Planned Time, Treated as Principal
Abaye's Rebuttal: Distinction Between Expected and Unexpected Drying
Case of Inherited Old Vines and Olive Trees: Support for Rava
Abaye's Concession and Alternative Explanation Regarding Ownership

macro_segments.title: The Disputed Years in a Mortgage Document: Possession, Burden of Proof, and the Status Quo Principle

micro_segments.title: Setup: Lost Mortgage Document with Unspecified Number of Years
Conflicting Claims: Creditor Says Three Years, Debtor Says Two
Rav Yehuda's Principle: Land Always in Owner's Presumed Possession (Karka Be-Chezkat Be'aleha)
Rav Kahana's Counterposition: Fruit in the Possession of Those Who Consumed It
Apparent Contradiction with Rav Nachman's Position on Rental Year Disputes
Resolution: Distinction Between Cases Where Evidence May Emerge and Cases That Are Final
Rationale: Avoiding Reversals of Court Decisions When Future Evidence Could Emerge

macro_segments.title: The Creditor's Credibility When the Loan Document Is Lost (Migo and Hiding the Document)

micro_segments.title: New Scenario: Five Years vs. Three Years with Document Lost Before Being Used
Rav Yehuda: Creditor Is Believed Due to Migo (Could Claim Purchase After Three Years)
Rav Zvid and Rav Avira Reject the Migo: Document Surely Not Lost, Intentionally Hidden
Creditor Always Careful with Loan Document Because Used for Collection
Concern About Abuse: Every Creditor Could Claim Purchase After Three Years
Protection Against Purchase Claims: Owner Must Pay Property Tax and Dig Trenches
For Land with No Tax or Maintenance Obligations: Owner Must Protest (Machaah) Annually

macro_segments.title: The Sharecropper Dispute: Presumption of Ownership vs. Local Custom

micro_segments.title: Setup: Sharecropper Claims Half Share, Landowner Claims One-Third
Rav Yehuda: Landowner Believed Because Land in His Possession
Rav Nachman: All Depends on Local Custom (Minhag Ha-Medina)
Clarification: Different Standards in Different Locations, Not a Debate
Abaye: Rav Yehuda's Possession Principle Overrides Local Custom
Landowner's Migo: Could Claim Workers Are Day Laborers, Not Sharecroppers

macro_segments.title: The Orphans and the Creditor: Liens, Improvements, and Who Bears the Burden of Proof

micro_segments.title: Setup: Father Dies with Debt; Question Whether Improvements Made Before or After Death
Rav Chanina's Initial View: Land in Orphans' Possession, Creditor Bears Burden of Proof
Rabbi Yochanan's Position: Due to Creditor's Lien, Orphans Must Prove Their Improvements
Lien Status (Apotiki): Specific Lien on One Property Makes Creditor the Presumed Possessor
Proof from the Tree-Cutting Case: Necessary to Cut Either Way, Burden on Claimant

macro_segments.title: Valuation of Improvements and Payment Methods: Cash vs. Land Division

micro_segments.title: Rav Chanina: Improvements Must Be Paid in Land (Divisible Property)
Correction: Creditor Takes Whole Land, Pays Orphans in Cash for Their Improvements
Source: Rav Nachman Quoting Shmuel on Three Evaluators and Valuing Benefits
Example: Firstborn and Regular Son Inheriting Land with Improvements
Application: Creditor, Wife (Ketubah), and Purchasers All Treated Uniformly
Distinction: Whether Improvements Reach the Value of the Loan or Exceed It
Scenario 1: Improvements Within Loan Amount—Creditor Takes All, No Payment
Scenario 2: Improvements Exceed Loan—Creditor Takes All Land, Pays Difference in Cash
Concern: Can Purchaser Pay Cash Instead and Stop Creditor From Taking Land?
Answer: Only in Special Case of Apotiki (Specific Lien) Can Purchaser Not Pay off Creditor

macro_segments.title: Rental and Sharecropping Agreements: Shmita Inclusion and Seven-Year Terminology

micro_segments.title: Renting for Seven-Year Period (Shavua) Including Shmita Year
Renting for Seven Planting Years Excluding Shmita

macro_segments.title: Obligations to Pay Hired Workers Immediately: Timing and Transgressions

micro_segments.title: Torah Sources: 'Lo Tavo Alav Ha-Shemesh' and 'Lo Talin Pe'ulat Sachir'
Day Workers: Can Collect Entire Following Night
Night Workers: Can Collect Entire Following Day
Hourly Workers: Can Collect All Night and Entire Following Day
Long-Term Workers (Week, Month, Year, Seven Years): Collection Based on Exit Time
Source Analysis: Lo Talin Only Transgressed Through One Morning, Not Continuously
Secondary Transgression After First Morning: Bal Teshahe (Delaying Wages) from Mishlei
Caveat: Bal Teshahe Only Applies if Employer Has Money Available
